nativeRange.habitat: Pristine blackwater tributaries and flooded forest margins of the upper Rio Negro basin near São Gabriel da Cachoeira, Amazonas state; tea-colored water with extremely low conductivity, very soft and acidic conditions, dark sandy substrate

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (sharp gravel damages barbels)
driftwood and leaf litter for tannins and cover
dense planting with open digging areas
dim lighting or floating plants to diffuse light
dark substrate to reduce stress and enhance coloration

diet.notes: The Adolfoi Cory forages along the bottom, sifting through substrate for food. It accepts high-quality sinking pellets, wafers, and tablets, and also readily takes small frozen and live foods such as bloodworm, Tubifex, daphnia, and Artemia. It should not be expected to clean up leftovers from tankmates — feed it directly with sinking foods so it receives adequate nutrition. Maintaining soft blackwater conditions improves long-term health and feeding response.

goodWith: small South American characins (cardinal tetras, rummy-nose tetras, ember tetras)

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer Nicklas Iversen (user Corydoras-adolfoi). 4128x2322 px. Two live specimens in a planted blackwater tank, clearly showing the orange head blaze, black nape saddle and black eye stripe; group shot suits this shoaling species.
(c) Corydoras-adolfoi. 1272x827 px. Close single individual, best view of the vivid orange head blaze and crisp black saddle; slightly soft focus so kept as a non-recommended detail shot.
(c) Corydoras-adolfoi. 2048x1152 px. Single specimen foraging on sand, different pose; diagnostic markings visible, a touch distant/soft so non-recommended.

summary: The Adolfoi Cory is a small blackwater catfish from the upper Rio Negro in Brazil, recognizable by its bright orange blaze and bold black saddle. Keep it in groups of six or more in soft, acidic water that replicates its natural habitat. The species was reclassified as Hoplisoma adolfoi in 2024, though the trade name Corydoras adolfoi remains widely used.
careNotes:
The Adolfoi Cory, now formally recognized as Hoplisoma adolfoi following a 2024 phylogenomic revision of the Corydoradinae subfamily, originates exclusively from the upper Rio Negro basin near São Gabriel da Cachoeira in Amazonas state, Brazil — one of the most ion-poor, tea-colored blackwater systems on earth. To keep this species in good long-term health, the aquarium should closely replicate these conditions: very soft water (gH 1–8, kH 0–4), acidic pH (ideally 5.5–6.5, tolerated to 7.0), low conductivity, and dim lighting. Tannins from driftwood or dried leaf litter help maintain water chemistry and reduce stress. Fine sand substrate is essential — sharp gravel erodes the delicate barbels, which are critical sensory organs for foraging, and barbel damage is a common health issue in improperly set-up tanks. A minimum group of 6 individuals is strongly recommended; this species is gregarious and will display more natural behavior and reduced stress in larger groups. Feed directly with sinking foods, as this fish should not be relied upon to pick up tankmates' leftovers.

Two species are frequently confused with the Adolfoi Cory at point of sale. The first is Hoplisoma duplicareum, previously known as Corydoras duplicareus — nearly identical in appearance but distinguished by a longer, broader black dorsal-saddle bar that extends further down the flank, and by serrations on the posterior edge of the pectoral-fin spine that require magnification to confirm; it is often sold mixed with or in place of adolfoi. The second is Brochis imitator, formerly Corydoras imitator, which differs in snout length and fin morphology and was placed in a separate genus by the same 2024 revision. Careful identification at purchase is advised. All members of the Corydoradinae subfamily possess locking pectoral and dorsal spines that can deliver a mild sting, and they can also secrete a skin toxin when stressed — handle with care and transport only alongside other Corydoradinae.
breedingNotes: Like most Corydoras relatives, the Adolfoi Cory is an egg-scatterer. Conditioning adults with a varied diet of live and frozen foods encourages breeding readiness. Spawning is typically triggered by performing large partial water changes of 50–70%, using slightly cooler, very soft, acidic water combined with increased surface agitation. A ratio of two males per female is commonly used. Eggs — typically 25–30 per spawning, deposited over the course of one to two weeks — are laid on glass surfaces, fine-leaved plants, or spawning mops. Adults should be removed after spawning to prevent them from eating the eggs. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–5 days at 24°C. First foods should be small enough for the tiny fry: infusoria, microworms, and freshly hatched Artemia nauplii are commonly used. Fry are delicate and require frequent small water changes to maintain quality. Breeding is considered moderately demanding due to the strict soft, acidic water parameter requirements and the sensitivity of the fry.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras adolfoi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (55–60 mm SL), temperature (20–26°C), pH (4.0–7.0), hardness (18–90 ppm as CaCO3), native range (upper Rio Negro near São Gabriel da Cachoeira, Brazil), habitat (blackwater tributaries and flooded forest), diet (omnivore, sinking foods plus small live/frozen), breeding protocol (2 male:1 female, large cool water change trigger, 3–4 day incubation), and confusion notes with C. duplicareus.

additional.name: FishBase — Hoplisoma adolfoi
additional.notes: Confirmed currently accepted name as Hoplisoma adolfoi (Burgess, 1982); adult size 5.7 cm SL; Negro River basin distribution; temperature 22–26°C; pH 6.0–8.0; family Callichthyidae, order Siluriformes; trophic level 3.0 (benthic/omnivore); IUCN Least Concern. Note FishBase pH range is broader than blackwater-specialist sources; SeriouslyFish/AquaInfo preferred for captive parameters.

additional.name: AquaInfo — Hoplisoma adolfoi
additional.notes: Confirmed confusion with C. duplicareus (broader stripe) and C. imitator (longer snout); adult size to 6 cm; temperature 22–26°C; pH 5.5–6.5; gH 5–8; minimum tank 80 cm length; minimum group 6–8; spawning 25–30 eggs over 2 weeks on glass and plants; noted locking spine toxin defense mechanism; named for Adolfo Schwartz, fish exporter from Manaus.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping UK — Corydoras Taxonomy Shake-Up
additional.notes: Confirmed 2024 Corydoradinae phylogenomic revision (Dias, Tencatt, Roxo et al., Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society): C. adolfoi → Hoplisoma adolfoi; C. duplicareus → Hoplisoma duplicareum; C. imitator → Brochis imitator. Seven genera now recognized in Corydoradinae: Corydoras, Aspidoras, Scleromystax, Gastrodermus, Osteogaster, Brochis, Hoplisoma.

additional.name: Wikimedia Commons — Corydoras adolfoi image category
additional.notes: Image source verification. High-resolution specimen photograph by Nicklas Iversen (CC BY-SA 4.0) used as imageCandidates entry.

fish.breedingNotes: An egg-scatterer that spawns at a ratio of two males per female. Breeding is triggered by large partial water changes with cooler, soft, acidic water combined with increased surface oxygenation. Eggs number around 25–30 per spawning, deposited on glass or plants over one to two weeks, and hatch in 3–5 days. Fry require small live foods such as microworms and Artemia nauplii. Strict soft, acidic water requirements throughout raise the breeding difficulty to moderate.
